title: The Unexpected Math Behind Van Gogh‚Äôs ‚ÄúStarry Night‚Äù
description:
in Art, Math |   	May 28th, 2026  1 Comment
	
			

	

If you‚Äôve tak¬≠en a good art his¬≠to¬≠ry course on the Impres¬≠sion¬≠ists and Post-Impres¬≠sion¬≠ists
body:
in Art, Math |   	May 28th, 2026  1 Comment
	
			

	

If you‚Äôve tak¬≠en a good art his¬≠to¬≠ry course on the Impres¬≠sion¬≠ists and Post-Impres¬≠sion¬≠ists, you‚Äôve inevitably encoun¬≠tered Vin¬≠cent van Gogh‚Äôs 1889 mas¬≠ter¬≠piece ‚ÄúStar¬≠ry Night,‚Äù which now hangs in the MoMA in New York City. The paint¬≠ing, the muse¬≠um writes on its web¬≠site, ‚Äúis a sym¬≠bol¬≠ic land¬≠scape full of move¬≠ment, ener¬≠gy, and light. The quiet¬≠ness of the vil¬≠lage con¬≠trasts with the swirling ener¬≠gy of the sky.‚Ä¶ Van Gogh‚Äôs impas¬≠to tech¬≠nique, or thick¬≠ly applied col¬≠ors, cre¬≠ates a rhyth¬≠mic effect‚Äîthe pic¬≠ture seems to con¬≠stant¬≠ly move in its frame.‚Äù Artis¬≠ti¬≠cal¬≠ly, van Gogh man¬≠aged to cap¬≠ture move¬≠ment in a way that no artist had ever quite done it before. Sci¬≠en¬≠tif¬≠i¬≠cal¬≠ly, it turns out, he was on to some¬≠thing too. Just watch the new TED-ED les¬≠son above, The Unex¬≠pect¬≠ed Math Behind Van Gogh‚Äôs ‚ÄúStar¬≠ry Night.‚Äù
Cre¬≠at¬≠ed by math artist/teacher Natalya St. Clair and ani¬≠ma¬≠tor Avi Ofer, the video explores how ‚ÄúVan Gogh cap¬≠tured [the] deep mys¬≠tery of move¬≠ment, flu¬≠id and light in his work,‚Äù and par¬≠tic¬≠u¬≠lar¬≠ly man¬≠aged to depict the elu¬≠sive phe¬≠nom¬≠e¬≠non known as tur¬≠bu¬≠lence. In Star¬≠ry Night, the video observes, van Gogh depict¬≠ed tur¬≠bu¬≠lence with a degree of sophis¬≠ti¬≠ca¬≠tion and accu¬≠ra¬≠cy that rivals the way physi¬≠cists and math¬≠e¬≠mati¬≠cians have best explained tur¬≠bu¬≠lence in their own sci¬≠en¬≠tif¬≠ic papers. And, it all hap¬≠pened, per¬≠haps by coin¬≠ci¬≠dence (?), dur¬≠ing the tur¬≠bu¬≠lent last years of van Gogh‚Äôs life.
Note: An ear¬≠li¬≠er ver¬≠sion of this post appeared on our site in 2014.
If you would like to sup¬≠port the mis¬≠sion of Open Cul¬≠ture, con¬≠sid¬≠er mak¬≠ing¬Ýa dona¬≠tion to our site. It‚Äôs hard to rely 100% on ads, and your con¬≠tri¬≠bu¬≠tions will help us con¬≠tin¬≠ue pro¬≠vid¬≠ing the best free cul¬≠tur¬≠al and edu¬≠ca¬≠tion¬≠al mate¬≠ri¬≠als to learn¬≠ers every¬≠where. You can con¬≠tribute through Pay¬≠Pal, Patre¬≠on, and Ven¬≠mo (@openculture). Thanks!

title: A Tour of Athens‚Äô Acropolis, Explained with 3D Reconstructions
description:
in Architecture, History |   	May 28th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Since it was first built as a Myce¬≠naean fortress in the thir¬≠teenth cen¬≠tu¬≠
body:
in Architecture, History |   	May 28th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

Since it was first built as a Myce¬≠naean fortress in the thir¬≠teenth cen¬≠tu¬≠ry BC, what we now know as the Acrop¬≠o¬≠lis has been used to wor¬≠ship not just Greek gods, but also, in lat¬≠er peri¬≠ods, the Vir¬≠gin Mary and Allah. Now, of course, with its days of mil¬≠i¬≠tary and reli¬≠gious func¬≠tions long behind it, it stands as a set of ruins. Still, they‚Äôre very pop¬≠u¬≠lar ruins, as evi¬≠denced by the crowds cap¬≠tured in the video above from Manuel Bra¬≠vo. Though most tourists at the Acrop¬≠o¬≠lis come with the idea that its build¬≠ings would have looked more glo¬≠ri¬≠ous in the dis¬≠tant past, few can have much of a sense of how to imag¬≠ine that with any accu¬≠ra¬≠cy. Using 3D mod¬≠els, Bra¬≠vo inte¬≠grates views of how the Parthenon, the Tem¬≠ple of Athena Nike, and oth¬≠er struc¬≠tures look now with how they would have looked in Athens‚Äô gold¬≠en age.
To ful¬≠ly appre¬≠ci¬≠ate the Acrop¬≠o¬≠lis requires not just an idea of how it was orig¬≠i¬≠nal¬≠ly intend¬≠ed to look, as Bra¬≠vo empha¬≠sizes, but also the inten¬≠tions of ancient Greek archi¬≠tec¬≠ture. The approach up the hill was meant to feel like an ascent from the mun¬≠dane world into the sacred one.
Enter¬≠ing the cen¬≠tral space on top, the vis¬≠i¬≠tor was led to view¬≠ing points that showed the sur¬≠round¬≠ing col¬≠lec¬≠tion of build¬≠ings at their most dra¬≠mat¬≠ic, a design the archi¬≠tects might have described as cin¬≠e¬≠mat¬≠ic, had cin¬≠e¬≠ma exist¬≠ed at the time. Even in its ruined state, the Acrop¬≠o¬≠lis still trans¬≠mits a sense of how, where, and to what degree that vis¬≠i¬≠tor was meant to be filled with awe, as well as where he was meant to look. And noth¬≠ing up there ‚Äî at least in the absence of Phidias‚Äô thir¬≠ty-foot stat¬≠ue Athena Pro¬≠ma¬≠chos ‚Äî draws atten¬≠tion as delib¬≠er¬≠ate¬≠ly as the Parthenon.
As we pre¬≠vi¬≠ous¬≠ly not¬≠ed here on Open Cul¬≠ture, if you make the trip to the Acrop¬≠o¬≠lis your¬≠self, you can now see¬Ýthe Parthenon with¬≠out scaf¬≠fold¬≠ing (or, depend¬≠ing on when you go, a min¬≠i¬≠mum of scaf¬≠fold¬≠ing) for the first time in 200 years. That lack of obstruc¬≠tion makes it eas¬≠i¬≠er to envi¬≠sion the glo¬≠ries of that cel¬≠e¬≠brat¬≠ed build¬≠ing back when it was both the tem¬≠ple of Athena and the trea¬≠sury of Athens. But as Bra¬≠vo says, if you real¬≠ly want to gaze upon the Parthenon as the ancients knew it, mar¬≠bles and all, you‚Äôll have to make the trek out to Nashville, Ten¬≠nessee,¬Ýwhere a full-scale repli¬≠ca was built in 1897 for the city‚Äôs Cen¬≠ten¬≠ni¬≠al Expo¬≠si¬≠tion. It may feel a bit odd to turn up in a place known for coun¬≠try music and bach¬≠e¬≠lorette par¬≠ties in search of the archi¬≠tec¬≠tur¬≠al, and per¬≠haps spir¬≠i¬≠tu¬≠al foun¬≠da¬≠tion of Europe. But then, civ¬≠i¬≠liza¬≠tion has nev¬≠er tak¬≠en a pre¬≠dictable course.

The City of Nashville Built a Full-Scale Repli¬≠ca of the Parthenon in 1897, and It‚Äôs Still Stand¬≠ing Today

Based in Seoul,¬ÝCol¬≠in¬ÝMarshall¬Ýwrites and broad¬≠casts on cities, lan¬≠guage, and cul¬≠ture. He‚Äôs the author of the newslet¬≠ter¬ÝBooks on Cities¬Ýas well as the books¬ÝÌïúÍµ≠ ÏöîÏïΩ Í∏àÏßÄ¬Ý(No Sum¬≠ma¬≠riz¬≠ing Korea) and¬ÝKore¬≠an Newtro.¬ÝFol¬≠low him on the social net¬≠work for¬≠mer¬≠ly known as Twit¬≠ter at¬Ý@colinmarshall.

title: Matte Marble Dust Glimmers Across Marin Majic's Enigmatic Scenes
description: Layers of colored pencil and marble dust worked into an oil-like substance flood the linen planes on which Marin Majic works. The Brooklyn-based a
body:
Layers of colored pencil and marble dust worked into an oil-like substance flood the linen planes on which Marin Majic works. The Brooklyn-based artist builds upon a foundational drawing, blending various media into a richly textured surface resembling fabric or plaster. Matte finishes radiate across the scenes, appearing like magical glimmers under a night sky.
Steeped in mystery, Majic‚Äôs works gravitate toward questions of power, impermanence, and the slippery nature of reality. Figures are often alone, whether swimming solo or driving along a mountain pass with no other cars in sight. Insects and animals are similar, although in pieces like ‚ÄúNegative attention,‚Äù we‚Äôre witness to the demise of the others, as a glinting spiderweb clings to a handful of colorful specimens. 
‚ÄúNegative attention‚Äù (2026), colored pencil, oil color, and marble dust on linen, 13 x 16 inches. Photo by Elisabeth Bernstein
Deep shadows and haze blanket the scenes, with small pockets of light offering only bits of insight into the full scope of the landscape. The enigmatic nature of Majic‚Äôs work also echoes that of his material choices: he utilizes the chalky marble dust to accentuate and define light, creating a twinkling effect with otherwise matte stone.
Majic is currently preparing for several group shows this summer, along with a book release with Nino Mier Gallery. Explore more of his work on Instagram.
Photo by Cary Whittier
‚ÄúWhile We Drive.‚Äù Photo by New Document
‚ÄúUnder the Radar.‚Äù Photo by Elisabeth Bernstein
‚ÄúNight Swim II.‚Äù Photo by Elisabeth Bernstein
Detail of ‚ÄúNight Swim II.‚Äù Photo by Elisabeth Bernstein
Detail of ‚ÄúSee You.‚Äù Photo by Elisabeth Bernstein

title: A Leonora Carrington Biopic Traces the Surrealist Icon's Life and Work
description: ‚ÄúDon‚Äôt you think it‚Äôs dangerous to blur the distinction between abstraction and reality?‚Äù asks actress Olivia Vinall in her role as the Surrealist
body:
‚ÄúDon‚Äôt you think it‚Äôs dangerous to blur the distinction between abstraction and reality?‚Äù asks actress Olivia Vinall in her role as the Surrealist artist and writer Leonora Carrington¬Ý(1917-2011). The heady line is one of the standout statements in the new biopic documenting Carrington‚Äôs life and work.
Directed by Thor Klein and Lena Vurma and produced by Modern Films, Leonora in the Morning Light opens in 1930s Paris, when the artist was enmeshed in an avant-garde community that included luminaries like Salvador Dal√≠ and Andr√© Breton, along with her partner Max Ernst. When World War II begins, Carrington flees to Spain before eventually re-settling in Mexico, perhaps the location most associated with her work.

The film is based on the biographical novel by Elena Poniatowska and comes at a time when Carrington‚Äôs oeuvre is in the spotlight, particularly the fantastical work made while she was confined to a psychiatric hospital in Spain which had been lost for 80 years.
Modern Films is also behind the documentary about pioneering artist Hilma af Klint, along with Boom For Real, which chronicles Jean-Michel Basquiat‚Äôs teenage years. Leonora in the Morning Light is slated for release on May 29 in the U.K. and Ireland.

schema.headline: A Leonora Carrington Biopic Traces the Surrealist Icon's Life and Work

title: Roda Medhat Subverts Traditional Kurdish Narratives Into Modern Tactile Experiences
description: Toronto-based Kurdish artist Roda Medhat pushes the boundaries of fabric into the realm of sculpture, exploring the ways in which traditional West
body:
Toronto-based Kurdish artist Roda Medhat pushes the boundaries of fabric into the realm of sculpture, exploring the ways in which traditional West Asian textiles can be translated into various media. As digital fabrication and 3D scanning cross paths with memory and material, Medhat‚Äôs practice asks ‚Äúhow we carry our stories, and what happens when those stories are translated into new, synthetic languages?‚Äù
The artist‚Äôs new solo exhibition, titled From the Loom, fills Toronto‚Äôs Abbozzo Gallery with large-scale sculptures in conversation with a new series of textile works. Known in part for his neon installations, the artist also presents several glowing light-based works encased within glass or acrylic, redolent of patterned Kurdish rugs.
‚ÄúDef‚Äù (2026). Photo by Fraser Carr Moore
Several of Medhat‚Äôs images and symbols‚Äîmost prominently young boys riding horses and interacting with nature‚Äîare sourced from Kurdish children‚Äôs books. These icons are woven directly into the surface of each textile by way of an electronic Jacquard machine, further accentuating the contrast between preserved cultural objects and contemporary reconstruction. In the exhibition‚Äôs statement, Medhat shares that his work ‚Äúfunctions as a distillation of a wider body of research,‚Äù including the contemporary subversion of archival materials.
‚ÄúThe Sheep and the Chevrolet,‚Äù an anchoring work within the exhibition, reimagines Fran√ßois Balsan‚Äôs problematic 1947 ethnographic work of the same title. Pitting bucolic Kurdish life with Western modernism, Balsan‚Äôs off-key travelogue presented a stereotypical, highly subjective view of Kurdish culture. Medhat‚Äôs bold sculpture invokes 3D printing to construct a monumental sheep composedly sitting atop a small Chevrolet vehicle, offering a playful point of reconceptualization.
From the Loom is on view through May 26. You can find more from the artist on Instagram.
‚ÄúThe Sheep and the Chevrolet‚Äù (2026). Photo by Darren Rigo
Photo by Darren Rigo
‚ÄúA Rug Falls in Four Frames‚Äù (2025). Photo by Fraser Carr Moore
Photo by Darren Rigo

‚ÄúBoy, Cat, Bike, Mother‚Äù (2026). Photo by Fraser Carr Moore
‚ÄúJajim 1‚Äù (2026). Photo by Fraser Carr Moore
Detail of ‚ÄúJajim 2‚Äù (2026). Photo by Fraser Carr Moore
Photo by Darren Rigo
Photo by Darren Rigo
Detail of ‚ÄúDef‚Äù (2026). Photo by Fraser Carr Moore

title: Christina Mrozik Illustrates an 'Emotional Ecosystem' Oracle Deck with Fantastical Forms
description: Six years in the making, Christina Mrozik‚Äôs new project tackles ‚Äúthe feelings of all things.‚Äù Fifty cards comprise the richly illustrated oracle d
body:
Six years in the making, Christina Mrozik‚Äôs new project tackles ‚Äúthe feelings of all things.‚Äù Fifty cards comprise the richly illustrated oracle deck, which delves into a vast emotional terrain through the artist‚Äôs distinctive visual metaphors of flora and fauna. Monochromatic in palette and surreal in subject matter, the individual works portray a variety of curious pairings from twin turtles with eyes nested into their shells to a bird speared by a spindly tree to a lily pad bursting from an open alligator jaw.
‚ÄúEach drawing took between 20 to 40 hours of focused work,‚Äù Mrozik writes. ‚ÄúI built every image in layers‚Äîstarting with rough sketches, researching forms, refining the composition, and slowly developing the final drawing in pencil until it felt complete.‚Äù

What results is an ‚Äúemotional ecosystem designed to help you witness your feelings, understand their purpose, and build a relationship with your inner world. It is not a tool for fixing yourself, but a practice for meeting yourself,‚Äù they add. 
As with much of Mrozik‚Äôs work, The Feelings of All Things melds beauty and pain, death and life. The deck is designed to be used on its own or alongside a preferred tarot set, either by selecting an illustration that feels most alluring or profound in the moment or simply choosing a card and reflecting on the imagery.
The Feelings of All Things has just under a week to go on Kickstarter, where it‚Äôs blown past its original goal, although there are still a few rewards available. Find more on Mrozik‚Äôs website and Instagram.

schema.headline: Christina Mrozik Illustrates an 'Emotional Ecosystem' Oracle Deck with Fantastical Forms

title: This Wood-Fiber Dress Was Made from a 17th-Century Shipwreck
description: Some of the most exciting designs emerging from the world of sustainable fashion are those utilizing uncommon materials. There are gowns sculpted
body:
Some of the most exciting designs emerging from the world of sustainable fashion are those utilizing uncommon materials. There are gowns sculpted with grass roots, sequins made from algae, and electrical wires woven into lace. Now, researchers and designers at Aalto University can add another unusual substance to that list: the remains of a 300-year-old wooden shipwreck.
In 2019, a hotel in the Finnish city of Oulu undertook renovations that uncovered a 17th-century vessel buried beneath a parking lot. Called the Hahtiper√§ wreck, the finding was the oldest of its kind in this region, prompting conservators to raise the seven-by-20-meter ship for preservation. A few fragments remained, though, and researchers from Aalto‚Äôs Bioinnovation Center seized the opportunity to save these bits from the trash.
According to UNESCO, wrecks can be raised and conserved for justified reasons. The Hahtiper√§ wreck was conserved because it is the oldest shipwreck discovered in Northern Finland. Photo by Minna Koivikko/Finnish Heritage Agency
After removing the outer layers, designers shredded and dissolved the wood into pulp. They then utilized their trademarked Ioncell process‚Äîdeveloped in collaboration with Helsinki University‚Äîwhich recycles materials like paper, straw, and other textile waste into silky fibers. 
Lecturer Anna-Mari Leppisaari was responsible for machine-knitting the undyed yarn into a pair of seamless dresses, one of which is on display at Oulu Art Museum for an exhibition about the future of fashion. A sleek A-line shape, the garment‚Äôs marbled pattern mimics that of wood grain. It weights less than a pound.
‚ÄúOf course, a shipwreck is an exceptional case, but it‚Äôs also a story that makes people pause and appreciate materials in a new way,‚Äù lead designer Pirjo K√§√§ri√§inen says. ‚ÄúIf something this beautiful can be made from centuries-old wood, why do we keep throwing away materials that could still be circulated and reused?‚Äù
The second dress will be on view in September for the university‚Äôs Designs for a Cooler Planet exhibition. (via The History Blog)

Shipwreck materials. Photo by Esa Kapila
Anna Mari Leppisaari knitting the dress. Photo by Anna Berg


Inge Schlapp making the fiber. Photo by Anna Berg
The preserved section was about seven meters wide and around twenty meters long. The part visible in the picture will be conserved and put on display in an exhibition at the Oulu Museum in the new museum and science center, Tiima. Phot by Minna Koivikko

schema.headline: This Wood-Fiber Dress Was Made from a 17th-Century Shipwreck
